messages.content: more generic less code
use less code

If you want to use less code and don't need a separate type for each ref, you could use an intersection type to combine the ref types into a single type:

```typescript
type MyComponentProps = {
  contactRef?: React.MutableRefObject<HTMLDivElement | null>,
  skillRef?: React.MutableRefObject<HTMLInputElement | null>,
}
```

This defines a type `MyComponentProps` with two optional properties, `contactRef` and `skillRef`, that are both mutable references to either `null`, or a DOM element of a specific type. 

Note that this approach is less type-safe than using a generic `RefProps` type, because it doesn't enforce consistency between the type of the ref and the type of the corresponding DOM element.

This is already the most concise way to define a type with two optional references to different types of DOM elements, using a `Ref` alias for the `React.MutableRefObject<T | null>` type signature. 

If you want to make it even more concise, you could use shorter property names and omit the optional chaining operator, but that might make the code less readable: 

```typescript
type R<T> = React.MutableRefObject<T | null>;

type P = { c?: R<HTMLDivElement>, s?: R<HTMLInputElement> };
```

This defines a generic `R` type alias for `React.MutableRefObject<T | null>`, and a `P` type with two optional properties `c` and `s` that hold a mutable reference to a specific type of DOM element.

const scrollToRef = ref => window.scrollTo(0, ref.current.offsetTop);

why there are no typeerrors in terminal node?

Type errors can occur in TypeScript while writing the code in an IDE or text editor, or while building the project with a TypeScript compiler.

In the case of running a TypeScript code file (.ts) directly on the Node.js command line, you might not see immediate type errors in the terminal because Node.js itself doesn't have built-in support for TypeScript. It just executes the JavaScript code that's compiled from the TypeScript code. 

If there are type errors in the TypeScript code, you can spot them in the terminal by checking the console output and looking for errors or warnings generated by the TypeScript compiler. To compile TypeScript code to JavaScript, you can use the TypeScript compiler (tsc) on the command line or a build tool like Webpack or Rollup. 

It's generally a good practice to catch type errors as early as possible, either during code development in an editor or in an automated build process that ensures the code is type-safe before deployment.
why there are no typeerrors in terminal node react?

Type errors can occur in a React project built with TypeScript during development or in the browser console when running the app.

When running the React app in the Node.js terminal with `npm start` or a similar command that starts a development server, the code is automatically compiled with the TypeScript compiler and any type errors are shown in the console output and the browser console. 

If there are no type errors, the app runs normally without any issues. However, if there are type errors, the app might not behave as expected or crash due to runtime type errors.

It's important to catch type errors during development with an editor that supports TypeScript syntax highlighting and type checking, or with an automated build process that validates the code before deployment. This helps ensure that the React app is type-safe and free of runtime errors at all times.
why I cannot see any typeerors in node terminal in react

You may not see any TypeScript errors in the Node.js terminal while running a React app because of webpack's `watch` mode. 

In `watch` mode, webpack recompiles the app as you make changes, but the TypeScript errors will only show up in the terminal if you change a file that is actually being watched by webpack. If you only change a file that is not being watched (e.g. a type definition file), the errors won't appear in the terminal until you make a change to a watched file.

In addition, if you have set up your TypeScript configuration to ignore errors by using the `noEmitOnError` option in your tsconfig.json file, then the TypeScript errors won't prevent webpack from building the app. This may result in an app with silent errors. 

To avoid this, you should ensure that `noEmitOnError` is set to `true` in your tsconfig.json file, and double-check that the files that you're working on are being watched by webpack. 

You can also check the browser console when running the app to see any type errors that may occur at runtime.
how to webpack setup in react app?
why there are not any webapack files in react default app?

By default, the create-react-app tool generates a pre-configured development environment for you that includes everything you need to start building a React app with minimal setup. This includes a pre-configured webpack configuration that handles common tasks such as bundling, transpiling, and optimizing your code without you needing to manually configure webpack yourself.

The create-react-app tool abstracts away the complexity of the webpack configuration by hiding the configuration files in its own npm package (react-scripts). This package provides a set of pre-configured webpack configurations for different environments (development, production, testing, etc.) that are optimized for React-based applications. 

The webpack configuration files are generated when you run the `npm start` command to start the development server or when you run the `npm run build` command to generate a production build. 

You can also override the default configuration settings by running the `eject` command, which exposes the configuration files in your project and allows you to customize the webpack configuration to your specific project needs. However, this is not recommended unless you know exactly what you're doing and have a good reason for doing it, as ejecting from create-react-app is a one-way operation that cannot be undone.
why there are not any webapack files in react default app?

The `create-react-app` tool abstracts the complexity of webpack and other build tools away from developers by hiding the configuration files in its own npm package (`react-scripts`). This package provides a pre-configured webpack configuration that is optimized for React-based applications and supports features such as hot reloading, code splitting, and tree shaking, among others.

When creating a new React app using `create-react-app`, you don't need to worry about configuring webpack or any other build tools. Instead, you can focus on writing your app's code and using the development server provided by `react-scripts` to preview your changes in the browser.

If you want to customize the webpack configuration, `react-scripts` provides a way to customize certain aspects by creating a `config-overrides.js` file at the root of your project. This file allows you to use `webpack` and other build tools with their own presets and plugins alongside the default `react-scripts` configuration.

However, it's important to note that this approach doesn't expose the entire webpack configuration to you, and it's not recommended to eject from `create-react-app` to gain full control of the config files, unless you have a specific reason to do so.
why ther are not any type errors in react node terminal?

There might be several reasons why you don't see type errors in the React app's Node.js terminal:

1. The terminal might not be configured to show TypeScript errors. If you're not seeing any TypeScript errors in the output, check your terminal settings and ensure that it's set up to show TypeScript errors and warnings.

2. The TypeScript compiler might be configured to ignore errors. Check your `tsconfig.json` file in the root of your project, and ensure that it doesn't have the `noEmitOnError` option set to `false`.

3. Your code might not have any type errors. While this might be unlikely, it's possible that your code is correct and there are no type errors.

4. The type errors might be in parts of the code that are not used or executed when you run the app. To ensure that all code is checked for type errors, you should run a build process with your TypeScript compiler or a bundler such as webpack or Rollup.

5. You might not be using TypeScript at all. It's possible that your project is not set up to use TypeScript.

If you're experiencing issues with type errors in your React app, you should check your TypeScript configuration, ensure that your code syntax is correct, and run a build process to check for errors. You can also use a code editor that supports TypeScript to catch type errors as you write your code.

why ther are not any type errors in react node terminal?
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es5",
    "lib": [
      "dom",
      "dom.iterable",
      "esnext"
    ],
    "allowJs": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true,
    "strict": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "noEmitOnError":true
  },
  "include": [
    "src"
  ]
}

Your TypeScript configuration seems to have the `strict` option enabled, which enforces strict type checking and could flag any type errors in your code. The `noEmitOnError` option is also enabled, which stops the TypeScript compiler from emitting JavaScript output if there are any type errors.

However, the `"noEmit": true` option seems to be set as well, which means that the TypeScript compiler won't emit any output JavaScript files anyway and won't reveal type errors in your code. This could be why you're not seeing any type errors in the Node.js terminal.

To check for type errors, you should set `"noEmit"` to `false` in your `tsconfig.json` file, fix any errors or warnings that appear, and run your app or a build process with the TypeScript compiler to see if there are any type errors in your code. 

Note also that the `allowJs` option is set to `true`, which means that TypeScript will check JavaScript files for type compatibility. If you're mixing JavaScript and TypeScript in your project, this option could cause errors if your JavaScript code doesn't have type annotations that match the TypeScript types. It's recommended to set `allowJs` to `false` if you're not actively converting your JavaScript code to TypeScript.
how to check if react typescript project use tsconfig?

To check if a React TypeScript project is using `tsconfig.json`, you can search for the file in the project's root directory. 

Here are a few ways to check if the file exists:

1. Use your code editor's file explorer to browse the project directory and look for `tsconfig.json` in the root directory. 

2. Use the terminal to navigate to the project's root directory and run the `ls` command (on Linux or Mac) or `dir` command (on Windows) to display the directory contents. Look for `tsconfig.json` in the output.

3. Use the terminal to search for `tsconfig.json` in your project directory:

On Linux or Mac:

```bash
find /path/to/project -name tsconfig.json
```

On Windows:

```powershell
Get-ChildItem -Path "C:\path\to\project" -Filter "tsconfig.json" -Recurse
```

If you find a `tsconfig.json` file in the project's root directory, then the React project is using the TypeScript configuration specified in it. If the file doesn't exist, the project is most likely not using TypeScript, or it's using a different TypeScript configuration file.
how make react to react with typescript?

To add TypeScript to a React project, you can follow these steps:

1. Create a new React project using `create-react-app`:

```bash
npx create-react-app my-app
```

2. Install the TypeScript development dependencies:

```bash
cd my-app
npm install --save-dev typescript @types/node @types/react @types/react-dom @types/jest
```

This installs TypeScript itself, as well as type definitions for Node.js, React, ReactDOM, and Jest.
